items.note: The fundamental sentence pattern. は (wa, written with the hiragana for 'ha') is the topic marker — it marks what the sentence is about. です is the polite copula ('to be'). 「私はニコラです」(Watashi wa Nikora desu) = 'I am Nicolas'. は often translates as 'as for ~' or 'speaking of ~'. The topic can be dropped entirely when context is clear, which is why Japanese has so many subject-less sentences.

The most notorious distinction in Japanese. は marks the topic (what you're talking about), が marks the grammatical subject (who does the action or has the property). 「私はすしが好きです」(I like sushi) — は marks 'I' as the topic, が marks 'sushi' as the thing being liked. Rule of thumb: use が for new information, questions, or emphasis; は for things already in context. Compare: 「誰が来ましたか」(Who came?) vs 「田中さんは来ましたか」(Did Tanaka-san come?).

を (pronounced 'o' in modern Japanese, written with the archaic wo hiragana) marks the direct object — the thing that receives the action of the verb. 「水を飲みます」(Mizu wo nomimasu) = 'I drink water'. を never starts a sentence and always comes immediately after the noun it marks. Fun fact: を is the rarest hiragana in modern Japanese — it appears almost exclusively as this particle.

に is the most versatile particle. It marks: (1) direction/destination — 「東京に行きます」(I go to Tokyo); (2) specific time — 「三時に来ます」(I come at 3 o'clock); (3) existence location — 「駅にいます」(I am at the station); (4) indirect object — 「友達に電話します」(I call a friend). Think of に as a precise pointer: a specific place, a specific time, a specific target.

で is the 'action' version of に for location: 「図書館で勉強します」(I study at the library) — the library is where the action happens. で also marks means or method: 「電車で来ます」(I come by train), 「日本語で話しましょう」(Let's speak in Japanese). Compare: 「公園にいます」(I am in the park) vs 「公園で遊びます」(I play in the park).

の links two nouns: the first modifies the second. Like an apostrophe-s in English: 「私の名前」(watashi no namae) = 'my name'. But more flexible: 「日本の文化」= 'Japanese culture' (culture of Japan), 「大学の研究者」= 'university researcher'. の can also nominalise verbs: 「走るのが好きです」= 'I like running' (literally 'running-thing is liked'). Very common and forgiving — when in doubt, try の.

も replaces は or が when meaning 'also'. 「私もです」(Watashi mo desu) = 'Me too'. 「コーヒーもお茶も飲みます」= 'I drink both coffee and tea'. When used with a negative verb, も becomes 'either/neither': 「私も行きません」= 'I won't go either'. も can also express surprise or emphasis: 「十年も勉強した！」= 'I studied for as much as ten years!'.

から = from (starting point in time or space). まで = until/up to (endpoint). 「東京から大阪まで」= from Tokyo to Osaka. 「九時から五時まで働きます」= I work from 9 to 5. から also means 'because': 「疲れたから寝ます」= 'I'll sleep because I'm tired'. The logic: both senses involve a starting point — a place, a time, or a reason.

Japanese forms questions simply by adding か to the end of a statement — no word order change needed. 「これは何ですか」(Kore wa nan desu ka) = 'What is this?' 「好きですか」= 'Do you like it?' In very casual speech, か is dropped and intonation rises instead. か is sometimes written with a question mark in casual writing, but formally, Japanese doesn't use question marks (the か is enough).

To negate a polite verb (〜ます form), change ます → ません. 「食べます → 食べません」(tabemasu → tabemasen) = don't eat. 「わかりません」= 'I don't understand' — the most useful negative. For adjectives: い-adjectives drop い and add くないです (「高い → 高くないです」= 'not expensive'). な-adjectives use ではありません (「好きではありません」= 'don't like').

Te-form of verb + ください = polite request. て-form is the connective form: 食べる → 食べて, 飲む → 飲んで, 書く → 書いて. 「ゆっくり話してください」= 'Please speak slowly'. 「ここに書いてください」= 'Please write here'. To ask someone NOT to do something: 〜ないでください. This is the most natural way to make requests in Japanese, more polite than the imperative.

english: may I ~? / is it ok to ~?
items.note: Te-form + もいいですか = asking permission. 「写真を撮ってもいいですか」= 'May I take a photo?' 「入ってもいいですか」= 'May I come in?' Answer yes: はい、どうぞ (hai, douzo = yes, go ahead). Answer no: ちょっと... (chotto... = hmm, a little...) — Japanese rarely say a flat no. This construction is essential in Japan where asking permission is valued.

Verb stem + たいです = 'I want to do ~'. Drop ます from the polite form to get the stem: 食べます → 食べ + たい = 食べたい (want to eat). 「日本語を話したいです」= 'I want to speak Japanese'. たい conjugates like an い-adjective: 食べたくない (don't want to eat), 食べたかった (wanted to eat). Important: たい expresses your own desire, not someone else's — for that, use 〜たがっています.

Dictionary form of verb + ことができます = can do. 「日本語を話すことができます」= 'I can speak Japanese'. こと nominalises the verb ('the thing of speaking'). A simpler alternative is the potential form: 話せます (hanasemasu = can speak). ことができます is slightly more formal and emphatic. できます alone = 'it is possible / I can do it'.

The existence verbs. あります = exists (inanimate things: objects, events, money, time). います = exists (animate beings: people, animals). 「駅の近くにコンビニがあります」= 'There is a convenience store near the station'. 「部屋に猫がいます」= 'There is a cat in the room'. The line can blur: plants use あります; robots use います (debated). When in doubt, inanimate = あり, living = い.

Plain form of verb/adjective + と思います = 'I think that ~'. と is a quotation particle (like quotation marks). 「おいしいと思います」= 'I think it's delicious'. 「行くと思います」= 'I think I'll go'. Incredibly useful for softening statements or expressing uncertainty — Japanese speakers use it constantly, even when quite certain, as a mark of humility. 思う (omou) = to think/feel.

english: is doing ~ / ongoing action or state
items.note: Te-form + いる/います has two main uses: (1) ongoing action: 「今、食べています」= 'I am eating now'; (2) resulting state: 「結婚しています」= 'I am married' (the state of having married). Context usually makes it clear. This is why Japanese sometimes seems to lack a present tense — 食べます can mean 'I eat' (habitual) or 'I will eat' (future), while 食べています specifically means 'I am eating right now'.

ましょう = 'let's do ~' (suggestion/invitation). 「行きましょう」= 'Let's go!'. 〜ませんか is slightly softer: 'Shall we ~?' / 'Won't you ~?'. 「一緒に食べませんか」= 'Shall we eat together?' — an invitation. ませんか is often used when asking someone out for coffee, lunch, etc. The か makes it a question, leaving room for the other person to decline gracefully.
